tEn: Mark Zuckerberg on Jiu-Jitsu, Coffee, and UFC
tZh: 马克·扎克伯格谈柔术、咖啡与 UFC
sEn: Mark Zuckerberg discusses his morning jiu-jitsu routine, aversion to coffee, and experiences at UFC events with Lex Fridman.
sZh: 马克·扎克伯格与 Lex Fridman 讨论他的晨间柔术习惯、对咖啡的排斥以及 UFC 经历。

consensus.en: AI will give everyone superintelligence, empowering creativity and productivity.
consensus.zh: AI将赋予每个人超级智能，赋能创造力和生产力。

consensus.en: People are smarter than media think; trust them to make good decisions.
consensus.zh: 人们比媒体认为的更聪明；相信他们能做出好的决定。

consensus.en: Future computing will blend physical and digital via holographic glasses.
consensus.zh: 未来计算将通过全息眼镜融合物理与数字世界。

consensus.en: Social media's social aspect builds relationships, which is positive for well-being.
consensus.zh: 社交媒体的社交方面能建立关系，对幸福感有积极影响。

consensus.en: Curing all diseases is possible within a century, accelerated by AI.
consensus.zh: 治愈所有疾病在一个世纪内是可能的，AI将加速这一进程。

consensus.en: Technology frees people from drudgery, allowing more time for creativity and fun.
consensus.zh: 技术将人们从苦差事中解放出来，让更多时间用于创造和娱乐。

contrarian.en: Socially awkward people often have more empathy and understanding than smooth communicators.
contrarian.zh: 社交笨拙的人往往比圆滑的沟通者更有同理心和理解力。

contrarian.en: College is more about social growth than job prep; student debt is a big issue.
contrarian.zh: 大学更多是关于社交成长而非职业准备；学生债务是个大问题。

contrarian.en: Raw-dogging reality (no caffeine or drugs) is now the most insane thing you can do.
contrarian.zh: 无咖啡因或药物地直面现实，现在成了你能做的最疯狂的事。

contrarian.en: The best predictor of success is building something people actually use, not media narratives.
contrarian.zh: 成功的最佳预测指标是构建人们真正使用的东西，而非媒体叙事。

contrarian.en: AI should be fun and personal, not just a tool for answering hard questions.
contrarian.zh: AI应该有趣且个性化，而不仅仅是回答难题的工具。

contrarian.en: The future is not a single utopia but a space of choice for individuals.
contrarian.zh: 未来不是单一的乌托邦，而是个人选择的空间。

turns.en: I have some tour dates to tell you about. Miami, Florida on May 10th. Cedar Rapids, Iowa on June 19th. St. Paul, Minnesota on June 20th. Fargo, North Dakota on June 21st. Rapid City, South Dakota on June 22nd. Winnipeg and Calgary in Canada. All tickets at theon.com. Please go through those links so you get accurate pricing. And I appreciate your support for the return of the rat tour. Today's guest is an entrepreneur, an inventor, a philanthropist. He is one of the richest men in the world, and a consensus of wealth and power exists in only so few people. He co-founded Facebook in 2004 when he was 19 years old, the company that turned into Meta, which is where we are today in their headquarters. I'm thankful to spend time and get to know Mr. Mark Zuckerberg. And I will find a song. You drink coffee, man, or no?
turns.zh: 我有一些巡演日期要告诉你。5 月 10 日佛罗里达州迈阿密，6 月 19 日爱荷华州锡达拉皮兹，6 月 20 日明尼苏达州圣保罗，6 月 21 日北达科他州法戈，6 月 22 日南达科他州拉皮德城，以及加拿大的温尼伯和卡尔加里。所有门票都在 theon.com 上。请通过那些链接获取准确价格。感谢你们支持“老鼠巡演”的回归。今天的嘉宾是一位企业家、发明家、慈善家。他是世界上最富有的人之一，财富和权力的共识只存在于极少数人身上。他于 2004 年 19 岁时共同创立了 Facebook，这家公司后来变成了 Meta，也就是我们今天所在的他们的总部。我很感激能花时间认识马克·扎克伯格先生。我先找首歌。你喝咖啡吗，兄弟？

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 不喝。

turns.en: Really?
turns.zh: 真的吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, you've had it? I have. Sometimes on vacation, I'll drink it recreationally. It's like every once in a while. Just like a celebration.
turns.zh: 是的。我是说，你喝过吗？我喝过。有时候度假时，我会偶尔喝一下，就像庆祝一样。

turns.en: Really?
turns.zh: 真的？

turns.en: Yeah. No, I just hate anything that messes with my system. I don't like any kind of chemicals or anything like that.
turns.zh: 是的。不，我只是讨厌任何扰乱我系统的东西。我不喜欢任何化学物质之类的。

turns.en: Oh, really? So you like to keep everything in equilibrium?
turns.zh: 哦，真的吗？所以你希望一切都保持平衡？

turns.en: Yeah. My sister gives me such a hard time about that. She's like, "You're just sitting there raw dogging reality." Wow. It's kind of true.
turns.zh: 是的。我姐姐为此总取笑我。她说：“你就那么干坐着，直面现实。”哇，这倒是真的。

turns.en: So you've had it before, but you just don't like it?
turns.zh: 所以你以前喝过，但就是不喜欢？

turns.en: Yeah. I don't like it.
turns.zh: 是的。我不喜欢。

turns.en: So when you get up in the morning, that's not your thing? Is there something you do?
turns.zh: 所以早上起床后，你不喝咖啡？有什么其他习惯吗？

turns.en: No. I wake up and I fight people.
turns.zh: 没有。我醒来就去跟人打架。

turns.en: Oh, you mean you wake up and do jiu-jitsu?
turns.zh: 哦，你是说醒来练柔术？

turns.en: Yeah. I probably wake up around 7 or 7:30 whenever the kids start making noise around the house. Sleep is done. Then I look at my phone and see all these things people are doing, like "You did what? Are you kidding me? I have to go deal with this." So I compose myself and go fight for two hours, re-enter myself. Then I can go deal with the stuff. That helps. It's almost like your coffee in the morning, rolling jiu-jitsu.
turns.zh: 是的。我大概 7 点或 7 点半醒来，孩子们开始在家里吵闹，睡眠就结束了。然后我看手机，看到人们做的各种事，比如“你干了什么？开什么玩笑？我得去处理。”于是我调整好自己，去练两个小时格斗，重新找回状态。然后我才能去处理那些事情。这很有帮助。就像你早上的咖啡一样，练柔术。

turns.en: And right now you're doing more striking?
turns.zh: 你现在更多练打击技？

turns.en: Yeah, it's really fun. I think it's the greatest sport. It's neurologically stimulating, good cardio, good strength. A little bit of a threat, so it keeps you on your toes. Not like just running. I used to run around the neighborhood, but running is not that thrilling. One cool thing about jiu-jitsu is you can lose a match with somebody, they can submit you, but you'll learn something along the way. A lot of times the guy submitting you also wants to help you learn. So you can lose and win at the same time. That's what's masterful about it.
turns.zh: 是的，非常有趣。我认为这是最棒的运动。它能刺激神经，是有氧运动，也能锻炼力量。还有一点威胁性，所以让你保持警觉。不像跑步那样。我以前在附近跑步，但跑步没那么刺激。柔术的一个酷点是你可以输给某人，他们降服你，但你会在这个过程中学到东西。很多时候降服你的人也想帮你学习。所以你可以同时输和赢。这就是它的精妙之处。

turns.en: Do you do it?
turns.zh: 你练吗？

turns.en: I don't do it as much as I would like to.
turns.zh: 我没有像我想的那样经常练。

turns.en: Striking like that?
turns.zh: 像那样练打击技？

turns.en: No, I never got into striking, but I would just do jiu-jitsu on the mats. It's so fun to do with friends. And some guy chokes you so hard and then you're just like, "God, it's a good day." It's a cup of coffee. If a big fella is squeezing you until you can't handle it, that's a cup of coffee. Better than caffeine for me.
turns.zh: 不，我从来没练过打击技，但我会在垫子上练柔术。和朋友一起练很有趣。有人把你勒得很紧，然后你就觉得“天哪，真是美好的一天。”这就像一杯咖啡。如果一个大块头把你挤到受不了，那就是一杯咖啡。对我来说比咖啡因更好。

turns.en: Is there a vitamin or some staple you keep in your diet if not caffeine?
turns.zh: 如果不喝咖啡因，你饮食中有什么维生素或主食吗？

turns.en: I drink a very large amount of protein. Creatine. Vitamin D, all that stuff.
turns.zh: 我喝大量蛋白质。肌酸。维生素 D，那些东西。

turns.en: But it's not your thing?
turns.zh: 但这不是你的爱好？

turns.en: No. Never been my thing.
turns.zh: 不是。从来不是我的爱好。

turns.en: Where did I see? Oh, we saw each other at the UFC. And you and your wife were there, right?
turns.zh: 我在哪里见过？哦，我们在 UFC 见过。你和你妻子都在，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. Which one was that? I'm trying to think. It wasn't 312. That was Alex fighting Magomed.
turns.zh: 是的。是哪一场？我在想。不是 312。那是 Alex 对阵 Magomed。

turns.en: Yeah, I was sad about that, man.
turns.zh: 是啊，那场我很难过，兄弟。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: Your wife, I could see she was really into it, head in her hand sometimes.
turns.zh: 你妻子，我看得出来她真的很投入，有时手托着头。

turns.en: Yeah. I think she would say she enjoys it, but I think she mostly goes to support me. She's into it. It's tough. We've gotten to know a bunch of the fighters, and when you see someone you know get hit or go down, that's tough. I've gotten to train with Volkanovski a few times. We were there at 298 when he fought Ilia. That was really tough.
turns.zh: 是的。我想她会说她喜欢，但我觉得她主要是去支持我。她挺投入的。这很艰难。我们认识了不少拳手，当你看到认识的人被打倒或击倒时，那很难受。我和沃尔卡诺夫斯基一起训练过几次。我们在 298 现场看他跟伊利亚打。那真的很难受。

turns.en: Did you get to walk out with him?
turns.zh: 你和他一起出场了吗？

turns.en: No. He asked me to walk out with him and I was like, "All right, this will be a cool experience." Then I'm just standing there while they're walking. Did you see that meme where he's passing off all his clothes and I'm just sitting there useless? That was a fun moment.
turns.zh: 没有。他让我和他一起出场，我说：“好吧，这会是一次很酷的经历。”然后我就站在那儿看他们走。你看到那个表情包了吗？他递出所有衣服，我就坐在那儿毫无用处。那是个有趣的时刻。

turns.en: Was that scary? Like, what do I do now?
turns.zh: 那吓人吗？比如，我现在该做什么？

turns.en: No, it was a fun moment. But watching him get hit by Ilia, it was right cage shot, three feet from us. He's a tough guy, so that was tough. After that, Priscilla was like, "Oh man, I don't know if I can go watch Volkanovski in person again." But we had friends over, it was fun watching him fight Diego. It was a great fight. Tough moments in rounds two and four. But his heart, man. The way he described it after, did you see the shot in round four where Diego grazed his eyelid with his glove? He lost his vision and he was just trying to survive with head movement.
turns.zh: 不，那是个有趣的时刻。但看着他被伊利亚击中，就在笼边，离我们三英尺远。他是个硬汉，所以那很难受。之后普里西拉说：“天哪，我不知道还能不能现场看沃尔卡诺夫斯基比赛了。”但我们请了朋友来，看他跟迪亚哥打很有趣。那是一场精彩的比赛。第二和第四回合有些艰难时刻。但他的意志力，兄弟。他事后描述的方式，你看到第四回合迪亚哥的手套擦过他眼皮的那一拳了吗？他失去了视力，只能靠头部移动试图存活。

turns.en: It's like watching an animal try to survive on one of those Animal Planet shows or something when you're cheering for the, you know, you're like, "Oh, it's not going to go well." But then he does good.
turns.zh: 这就像看动物星球节目里动物求生一样，你心里想“哦，这下完了”，结果它却表现很好。

turns.en: Yeah. No. Um, he's an amazing guy. Um, he's really cool. Very talented. A lot of heart. Oh, yeah. And you get to know some of the fighters. I think that's one thing that is great about social media these days, that you get to know the fighters a little bit more, some of their regular life. Like you can get involved in who they are. So that gives you so much more of a person to cheer for.
turns.zh: 是啊。不，他是个了不起的人。他真的很酷，非常有才华，很有心。哦，是的。而且你能了解一些拳手。我觉得这是如今社交媒体很棒的一点，你能更了解拳手们，了解他们的一些日常生活。你可以参与到他们是谁中去。这样你就有了更多可以为之加油的人。

turns.en: Is it tough to take your wife since you're a, and I'm just going to go for it. Since you're a, I'm going to say the word really fast just so it's like, so we don't dwell on it, but since you're a billionaire, is it tough to take your wife on a date? Like, do you have to live up to a standard or what's like a nice date night? What is that like?
turns.zh: 带老婆出去约会难不难？因为你是——我就直接说了。因为你是，我快速说这个词免得纠结——既然你是亿万富翁，带老婆约会难吗？比如，你是不是得达到某种标准？或者一个美好的约会之夜是什么样的？

turns.en: Um, I don't know. No, I think she's pretty chill. Um, she's a chillionaire. Yeah, sorry. My niece will like it. Yeah, there you go. Um, no, I mean, I think the main thing for me is like life is busy. There's a million things that I could be doing at any given point in time. I just think it's important to take time, you know, each week. Like, Wednesday night, we really try to have a date night. Um, try to hang out with the kids and put them to bed every night. It's like that. That's just an important part of my routine. I think that's important stuff to do. But no, I mean, we try to go out somewhere, but every once in a while we'll just cook or eat at home and that's all good. The UFC stuff I think is probably more for me than for her. But she's a good sport about it.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不知道。不，我觉得她很随和。嗯，她是个“千万富翁”。是的，抱歉。我侄女会喜欢这个说法。嗯，不，我的意思是，对我来说主要就是生活很忙。任何时刻我都有无数事情可以做。我只是觉得每周抽出时间很重要。比如，周三晚上我们尽量安排约会之夜。嗯，每晚陪孩子玩、哄他们睡觉。就是这样。这是我日常的重要部分。我觉得这些都是重要的事。但不过，我们也会出去吃，偶尔就在家做饭或吃，也挺好。UFC 那些可能更多是我喜欢，不是她。但她很配合。

turns.en: Is there something you'll do for her, like a fancy date, because you can afford to take your wife on a date that most people could only dream of? Is there some magical date that you took her on one time?
turns.zh: 有没有为她做过什么，比如一次豪华约会，因为你能带老婆去大多数人梦寐以求的约会？有没有哪次你带她去了什么神奇的约会？

turns.en: Um, I don't know. That is a good question. Let's see. She's pretty simple on this stuff. I think most of the, you know, I like doing, I like making things, right? So, I don't know if you saw this thing. I like working with great artists and stuff. So, I did this project where I've always admired Daniel Arsham and he's this great sculptor, and I worked with him to make this sculpture of Priscilla because I thought it was cool. And, um, yeah, you're pulling this up. First of all, I think it's like, make a sculpture of Priscilla partially because that's cool, partially because I'm not going to make a sculpture of myself. It's like that's crazy, right? It's like who the does that? But like, so it's like she's kind of the target of my creative energy in a way. So there's all these memes online after, where people like, "Wow, I wonder what Zuckerberg did wrong that he had to make a sculpture of his wife." And I'm like, "No, you guys are totally missing this. This is the thing I did wrong. It's like you're going to have to wait to see what I have to do to make up for having made a sculpture and putting it on our front lawn." And it's like she doesn't want a sculpture of her in the front lawn. It's like that's weird, right? But, um, no, but she's a good sport about it. And at least you can tell the Door Dash guy, "Just set it by the sculpture." Yeah. That's kind of nice. It becomes like a, and I think having like a woman as a lookout in your yard is kind of nice, you know? Yeah. No, it's got like a good angelic form factor thing. It's a good vibe.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不知道。这是个好问题。让我想想。她在这类事情上很简单。我觉得大部分——你知道，我喜欢做东西，对吧？所以，不知道你有没有看到这个。我喜欢和伟大的艺术家合作。所以我做了个项目，我一直很欣赏 Daniel Arsham，他是位了不起的雕塑家，我和他一起为 Priscilla 做了个雕塑，因为我觉得很酷。嗯，对，你正在调出图片。首先，我觉得给 Priscilla 做雕塑，一部分是因为酷，一部分是因为我不会给自己做雕塑。那太疯狂了，对吧？谁会干那种事？但所以，她在某种程度上成了我创意的目标。之后网上有很多梗，人们说：“哇，Zuckerberg 到底做错了什么，要给老婆做个雕塑？”我说：“不，你们完全搞错了。这才是我做错的事。你们得等着看我得做些什么来弥补做了个雕塑还放在前院。”而她根本不想要前院有她的雕塑。那很奇怪，对吧？但不过，她很配合。至少你可以跟 Door Dash 外卖员说：“就放在雕塑旁边。”是啊，还挺好的。它变成了一个——我觉得院子里有个女性雕像还挺不错的，你知道吗？是啊，它有种天使般的外形，感觉很好。

turns.en: Did she make you make any adjustments to it or was she like, "Okay, I'll accept it as is."
turns.zh: 她有没有让你做任何修改，还是说“好吧，我就接受它本来的样子”？

turns.en: Um, no. I mean, I don't think she was that happy with it. Um, because again, I mean, like, who wants a sculpture of themselves in the front yard? But, um, but, you know, I think she thinks it's sweet and I think she appreciates that. Um, I mean, there's a lot of more destructive things I could be doing with my creative energy. Yeah. You made an effort, too. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, and so it's like I designed her this, um, this, uh, Porsche minivan thing. It's like we took like a Cayenne and extended it to be bigger. And it's like I do that because it's like, all right, I like cars, but I'm not going to design a super car for myself. Let's just design a sweet minivan for my wife, right? It's like, yeah, it was just fun. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So, I guess creative stuff like that, trying to be creative and show her some creativity.
turns.zh: 嗯，不。我觉得她没那么高兴。嗯，因为再说一次，谁会想要前院有自己的雕塑？但，嗯，不过，你知道，我觉得她觉得这很贴心，也欣赏这份心意。嗯，我的意思是，我可以用我的创意做很多更有破坏性的事情。是啊，你付出了努力。是啊。所以，我还给她设计了一辆保时捷的迷你货车。就像我们把 Cayenne 加长变大。我这么做是因为，好吧，我喜欢车，但我不会给自己设计一辆超级跑车。那就给老婆设计一辆漂亮的迷你货车吧，对吧？是啊，就是好玩。所以，我想就是这类创意的事情，努力发挥创意，向她展示一些创意。

turns.en: Oh, that's nice. There you go. I mean, look at that. And where did you meet your wife at?
turns.zh: 哦，那真好。就是这样。你看那个。你在哪里认识你妻子的？

turns.en: Um, I met her in school. So, at we were in college and I had just done this prank. Um, and all my friends and everyone were convinced they were all convinced I was going to get thrown out of school.
turns.zh: 嗯，我在学校认识她的。当时我们在上大学，我刚搞了个恶作剧。嗯，我所有的朋友和每个人都确信我肯定会被开除。

turns.en: And where were you at in school at, Mark?
turns.zh: 你在哪个学校，Mark？

turns.en: Harvard.
turns.zh: 哈佛。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: And so, so I was a sophomore, she was a freshman. Um, my friends were convinced I was about to get kicked out of school. I was like going in front of this like trial for the kind of like school discipline committee. Um, my friends threw a going away party for me and it was in the bathroom line at the end of the party. Um, where I was just like next to Priscilla and we were talking and you had nothing to lose. It was your last meal. I was just like, "Look man, it's you know, have—" I didn't say man, but it's um, like it was not very romantic situations. It's like we're waiting in line for the bathroom. She's just funny. She's cute. And it's like I was like, "All right, hey, you know, if we're going to go out, we better go do this quickly because I'm probably going to get kicked out of school in like two or three days." It's like that's a good pickup line, right? It's like, yeah, that's how you really show that you have potential in the world, right? It's, you know, it's—

transcript.secZh: 育儿与向孩子解释科技

turns.en: I think it's just good for them to have good role models, right? Part of the way I feel is when kids grow up, they either end up wanting to marry people who are the opposite of their parents if it was a bad experience, or people who they think of as a good role model. So I feel as long as they look up to us, that's what you want to set a good example. But for me, we talked about this a bit with the fighting stuff early on, but it's always been important that I don't just want to be a person who sits and works all the time. I think we're not meant to just sit at a desk all day long, and one day Oreo will learn that. A lot of life is moving around, being active, and doing stuff. That's a big part of it. It's important to me that the kids get that too, and they are very active.
turns.zh: 我认为对孩子来说，有好榜样是件好事，对吧？我的感觉是，孩子长大后，要么想嫁给与父母相反的人（如果童年经历不好），要么想嫁给他们认为的好榜样。所以只要他们仰慕我们，那就是你想树立的好榜样。但对我来说，我们之前聊过一点关于格斗的事，我一直觉得重要的是，我不想成为一个整天坐着工作的人。我觉得我们生来就不是整天坐在办公桌前的，总有一天 Oreo 会明白这一点。生活很大一部分是活动、运动和做事。这是很重要的一部分。我希望孩子们也能体会到这一点，他们确实非常活跃。

turns.en: Do your kids have a lot of screen time? How much screen time do you allow your kids?
turns.zh: 你的孩子有很多屏幕时间吗？你允许他们有多少屏幕时间？

turns.en: It's different for each one. We don't just let them do whatever, but I actually want them to be fluent with this stuff, like we talked about earlier. I want them to learn how to code and use technology. I think it's important because a lot of socialization happens online at this point. People need to get used to the norms around that. They're still too young to be using social media, but they have Messenger Kids. We make it so they can video chat and chat with their friends, and we monitor to make sure they're only connecting with people we think they should. I think it's actually good. People need to grow up learning how to use this stuff, especially with an engaged parent. I want the kids, to the extent they're interested, to learn how to code, create stuff in Horizon or VR tools, play Roblox and Minecraft. That's good. There are also educational study tools. When our daughter studies for competitions, she can make progress on that. So I think that stuff is good, but we're not just letting them sit and watch stuff all day long.
turns.zh: 每个孩子情况不同。我们不会让他们随心所欲，但我确实希望他们能熟练使用这些东西，就像我们之前聊的。我希望他们学会编程和使用技术。我认为这很重要，因为现在很多社交都在网上进行。人们需要适应相关的规范。他们还太小，不能用社交媒体，但他们有 Messenger Kids。我们让他们可以视频聊天和文字聊天，并监控确保他们只和我们认为合适的人联系。我觉得这其实是好事。人们需要在成长过程中学会使用这些东西，尤其是有父母引导的情况下。我希望孩子们，只要他们有兴趣，就学会编程、在 Horizon 或 VR 工具里创作、玩 Roblox 和 Minecraft。这很好。还有很多教育学习工具。我们女儿准备比赛时，可以用这些工具取得进步。所以我觉得这些都不错，但我们不会让他们整天坐着看东西。

turns.en: Unless you're on a plane, then you do whatever you need to get through that flight.
turns.zh: 除非在飞机上，那你就得想尽办法熬过飞行。

turns.en: Yeah. Thank you, dude. Somebody else needs to say that.
turns.zh: 没错。谢谢你，兄弟。这话得有人说。

turns.en: Where's the pacifier that really shuts these kids down for a couple hours? I'm not saying anything crazy, nothing illegal, but we need a high voltage pacifier back in society, Mark. Give us something, brother. These kids are screaming. The craziest thing happened to me. One time I'm on a plane, I'm dreaming, and I'm having some nice dreams because I never have good sleep. Every now and then I come to the surface of my dreams and go back underwater. But I heard a ukulele playing. At some point I wake up and there is a child playing a ukulele with his parents on this plane. I walked up and put my hand on it in front of the parents. I was still groggy and said, "We can't do this today." The ukulele is a lot. Time out for ukulele. We're shutting down this little hand Hawaii you got going on. It probably took a lot of restraint not to smash it. It really did. So I just said we're not doing this today in a tone that seems stern for a child, especially one who is not your child. I got lucky. The parents agreed. They were probably pissed about the ukulele too, afraid to tell their kids. So you did them a favor.
turns.zh: 那个能让孩子安静几个小时的安抚奶嘴呢？我不是说要什么疯狂的东西，不违法，但我们需要一个高压安抚奶嘴回归社会，马克。给我们点东西吧，兄弟。这些孩子太吵了。我遇到过最疯狂的事。有一次在飞机上，我在做梦，做着美梦，因为我从来睡不好。我时不时浮出梦境，又沉下去。但我听到了尤克里里声。后来我醒来，发现有个小孩在飞机上和他父母一起弹尤克里里。我走过去，当着父母的面把手按在琴上。我还迷迷糊糊的，说：“今天不能弹这个。”尤克里里太吵了。尤克里里暂停。我们要关掉你这小小的夏威夷表演。没把琴砸了真是克制了。真的。所以我就用对孩子来说很严厉的语气说今天不弹了，尤其那还不是你的孩子。我运气好，父母也同意了。他们可能也对尤克里里烦透了，只是不敢跟孩子说。所以你帮了他们一个忙。

turns.en: Yeah. It's like, "Hey, Oreo, sometimes business is closed." It's like working hours are over for today. You'll be back in the office tomorrow. Sometimes you just have to. So I agree. Please do whatever you can to stop these kids from being on planes or screaming on planes, because we are looking to you for that.
turns.zh: 没错。就像，“嘿，Oreo，有时候生意关门了。”今天的工作时间结束了。明天再回办公室。有时候就得这样。所以我同意。请尽你所能阻止这些孩子在飞机上吵闹，因为我们指望你了。

turns.en: Well, maybe one day we'll deliver that.
turns.zh: 好吧，也许有一天我们会实现这个。

So you can... glasses I think are like the perfect form factor for a device where if you want to have an AI that you let see what you see, hear what you hear, it can talk to you, you can talk to it throughout the day. And there's like... there's like a billion or two billion people in the world who wear glasses already. M so to me the... you know the chance that we look back like a decade from now and like all those glasses aren't AI glasses by that period. I think that's going to happen with um with glasses too.
turns.zh: 嗯，不，这算是……我是说这些是今天就能买到的。这些就像 REI 眼镜。所以，我的意思是，它们是眼镜，可以……嗯，你知道，主要功能是……嗯，你可以用它们拍照或录像。嗯，我很喜欢用它们听音乐和接电话，因为你的耳朵是开放的，对吧？所以它不会……不会遮挡你听到其他声音的能力。嗯，音质非常好，对吧？因为它有一个麦克风，就像一个接触式麦克风，基本上在鼻托里。所以当我……是的。所以当我像……你可以在飞机上接电话，电话那头的人甚至听不到你在飞机上。就像……你可以在风洞里，它也无所谓。音质太棒了。但主要的是，嗯，它们是 AI 眼镜。所以你可以……我认为眼镜是设备的完美形态，如果你想让 AI 看到你所见、听到你所听，它能和你说话，你也能整天和它说话。嗯，眼镜就是……你知道，是的，如果你想要一个拥有和你相同世界语境的东西，那就会是眼镜。而且世界上已经有十亿或二十亿人戴眼镜了。嗯，所以对我来说，你知道，十年后我们回头看，那些眼镜在那时都会是 AI 眼镜。这有点像……显然所有翻盖手机都会变成智能手机，对吧？我的意思是，那显然是会发生的事。我认为眼镜也会发生同样的事。

turns.en: But the um the other piece of this is that you're going to get the ability to kind of put holograms in the world, right? So our our experience with technology today is um I don't know it's kind of funny in a way how it's divided where it's like you... you know we have the physical world all around us and then if you want to interact with something digital you need to like put a screen up right so maybe it's like this you know you have your your small glowing rectangle your phone with you you know you you have like you know your screen if you want to like project something yeah your computer but I I think in the not too distant future this should be blended together right you'll like the physical world, but all this digital stuff should just basically be holograms. You shouldn't need like a physical screen. Um like there's no reason why in the future, you know, you want to have a screen there. You'll just have glasses and that screen will be a hologram, right? And that's what you that's what you put me in. Yeah. Somebody put me in it. Yeah. Yeah. That's final season of Stranger Things back there. I got put in whatever they put on me. It was like Lens Crafters makes lens crafters look pretty, you know, lame.
turns.zh: 但嗯，另一部分是，你将获得在现实世界中放置全息图的能力，对吧？所以我们今天与技术的体验是……嗯，我不知道，有点好笑，它是分割的，就像……你知道我们周围是物理世界，然后如果你想与数字内容互动，你需要竖起一块屏幕，对吧？所以可能是这样，你知道你带着你的小发光矩形——你的手机，你知道你有你的屏幕，如果你想投射什么东西，是的，你的电脑。但我认为在不久的将来，这应该会融合在一起，对吧？你会喜欢物理世界，但所有这些数字内容基本上都应该是全息图。你不需要物理屏幕。嗯，未来没有理由你需要在那里放一块屏幕。你只需要戴上眼镜，那块屏幕就会是一个全息图，对吧？那就是你让我体验的东西。是的。有人让我戴上了它。是的。是的。那是《怪奇物语》的最后一季。我戴上了他们给我的任何东西。它让 Lens Crafters 看起来相当……你知道，逊色。

And is static. Yeah, I think everything is going to be able to... Sorry, not everything, but it's an interesting thought experiment how much of the stuff that we physically have today just doesn't actually need to exist in the future.
turns.zh: 哦，是吗？是的。然后在我们的未来，不是每个人都拿奖牌。对。我喜欢这样。你不是吗？就像……对，那更……奥利奥，把烤架点起来。对。输家负责烤。但说真的，我们可以玩一局……而且那不是真的。我们面前有一张乒乓球桌，但不是真的。就像 3D 乒乓球。对。所以它并不存在。嗯哼。有人可以骑着自行车穿过，就像你那个傻兄弟骑着他那该死的自行车。真见鬼。对，瑞奇，兄弟，你他妈的直接穿过了我们的网。所以，我觉得这是个有趣的思维实验。我们拥有的实体物品有多少在未来是不需要的，对吧？所以，几乎所有的屏幕都不需要存在，对吧？它们只会是全息图。任何媒体、你玩的任何书、任何棋盘游戏、纸牌，那些都会很好，因为我讨厌的一点是机场里所有的电视都开着。现在一切都太吵了。就像，你能不能只为你自己播放？调低音量。为什么我们都要经历这个？对。没错。有时候真是痛苦的噪音。对。就像你开车穿过城市，有广告牌，但没有任何个性化。就像以前的电视，对吧？我的意思是，在未来，现在你手机上用的所有东西，你都能得到你感兴趣的内容，体验质量高得多，但所有这些实体东西都还留着，对吧？而且是静态的。对，我觉得一切都会……抱歉，不是一切，但这是个有趣的思维实验：我们今天拥有的实体东西有多少在未来其实不需要存在。

turns.en: So do you think social media is bad? I mean I don't think so but yeah maybe that's not the right term. Like at a certain point does... Like how much, you know, there's been studies done where it's like doom scrolling and stuff like that can lead to depression, that sort of thing. Like do you... What do you think about that?
turns.zh: 那么你觉得社交媒体是坏的吗？我觉得不是，但也许这个词不对。比如到了某个点……你知道，有研究说，像 doom scrolling 之类的东西会导致抑郁，诸如此类。你觉得呢？你怎么看？

turns.en: So look, we obviously study this stuff pretty carefully.
turns.zh: 听着，我们显然在非常仔细地研究这些东西。

turns.en: You guys do?
turns.zh: 你们研究？

turns.en: Yeah. We study it. We work with academics to study it. As you can imagine, there's a lot of media coverage of this stuff that's very sensationalist that tries to have a skewed point of view. My understanding of the current state of the research is that there isn't a conclusive finding that this is negative for people's well-being. So, I think that in general, some of the stuff that ends up being positive for people is building relationships. So there's the media part of social media and the social part of social media. I think the interacting with people, to the extent that that's helping you build good relationships, friendships and good relationships is one of the things that correlates the most strongly with positive well-being and feeling good about your life. The media stuff, I think that's more entertaining. People want things that are fun, right? People want to be entertained for sure. It doesn't necessarily correlate with good well-being or bad well-being. But I guess the way that I think about this stuff is that our modern online environment is just an environment in which we live, in a way that has pros and cons, like whether you live in a city or a rural place. Some people may prefer living in a city. There are things that are good about a city. There are things that are bad about a city. The fact that we went from being primarily offline as a society to now this kind of hybrid physical and digital reality, there are some things that are better about that, some things that are maybe less good. It's not that every single thing improves at each step along the way, but I think overall the overall effect is significant improvement. And I just don't see a way that people would want to go back to not having services where they can get the content that they're interested in and where stuff is personalized to them and where they can communicate with the people who they want and don't need to be constrained to just the people who are physically around them. We're not going back to that. If you look throughout history, when people first built cities, there was a lot of nostalgia for the simpler life. Maybe cities aren't better in every single way, and some people might prefer to only go sometimes, but we're building more and more capabilities as society. I just think about this stuff more as an environment in which we live.
turns.zh: 是的。我们研究它。我们与学者合作研究。你可以想象，有很多媒体报道非常耸人听闻，试图带有偏见。我对当前研究状况的理解是，没有确凿的发现表明这对人们的幸福感是负面的。所以，我认为总的来说，一些对人们有积极影响的东西是建立关系。所以社交媒体有媒体部分和社交部分。我认为与人互动，如果它能帮助你建立良好的关系，友谊和良好关系是与积极幸福感以及生活满意度最相关的因素之一。媒体部分，我觉得那更娱乐。人们想要有趣的东西，对吧？人们当然想要被娱乐。它不一定与好或坏的幸福感相关。但我想我对这些东西的看法是，我们现代的在线环境只是我们生活的一个环境，有利有弊，就像你住在城市还是乡村。有些人可能更喜欢住在城市。城市有好有坏。我们从社会主要在线下，到现在这种混合的物理和数字现实，有些方面更好了，有些可能没那么好。并不是每一步每件事都改善了，但我认为总体效果是显著的改善。我只是看不到人们会想回到没有服务、无法获取感兴趣的内容、没有个性化、无法与想联系的人沟通、只能局限于身边人的状态。我们不会回到过去。如果你纵观历史，当人们最初建造城市时，有很多对简单生活的怀旧。也许城市并非在每方面都更好，有些人可能只偶尔去，但我们在作为社会不断构建更多能力。我只是把这些东西更多地看作是我们生活的环境。

turns.en: Like some, I think because I've been thinking a lot about this. I think a lot of people have. It's like we're moving into such a... technology is advancing so much faster, right? It's accelerating the advancement speed and then we're just humans and we're in this space now where there's one generation to the next where one generation was completely grown up online and one hasn't really. So I wonder, and I think the older generation probably sees it as like man this is so negative everybody's stuck, but I wonder if the younger generation do they even know any different, right? You know, I start to wonder what is the value of being human, like what is the... does that start to dissipate as we become more technologically advanced or does that alter? Do you think about that sometimes?
turns.zh: 就像有些人，我想因为我一直在想这个。我想很多人都在想。就像我们正在进入一个……技术发展快得多，对吧？它在加速进步，而我们只是人类，现在处在一个代际之间，一代人完全在网上长大，另一代则不然。所以我想，老一辈可能觉得这太负面了，每个人都陷进去了，但我想知道年轻一代是否知道有什么不同，对吧？我开始想，作为人的价值是什么？随着技术越来越先进，这种价值是开始消散还是改变？你有时会想这个吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I think it's going to give people the freedom to focus more on the things that they want. I think if you look at the arc of history, go back to ancient times, life was pretty brutal, right? Then you go to maybe right before the industrial revolution where they had team toilet paper at one time, they had group toilet paper and it was like... That's not good.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为它会给人自由，让他们更专注于自己想做的事。如果你看历史的轨迹，回到古代，生活相当残酷，对吧？然后到工业革命前夕，他们一度有团队厕纸，有集体厕纸，那就像……那不好。

turns.en: I don't think anyone who has nostalgia for the past is really taking into account the disease and the lack of hygiene that existed. Pre-industrial revolution, a huge percent of the population was farmers because everyone needed to grow food. Then we got to the point where we could produce food much more efficiently, freeing up people to do other stuff. Now maybe 2% of people can be farmers and 98% can do other creative things. Being a farmer is a really hard job, working long hours. As people got more options, they took other jobs. The percent of time spent on leisure and entertainment has steadily increased over time. I think that will continue with technology. Basic needs will be taken care of, freeing people to do more creative jobs or not work as hard if they don't want to. But some people will like working all the time like me, and you'll be able to get more done than ever before. That will be a choice.
turns.zh: 我不认为任何怀念过去的人真的考虑了过去存在的疾病和卫生条件差的问题。工业革命前，很大一部分人口是农民，因为每个人都需要种植食物。后来我们能够更高效地生产食物，解放了人们去做其他事情。现在可能只有 2% 的人是农民，98% 的人可以做其他创造性的事情。农民是一份非常辛苦的工作，工作时间很长。随着人们有了更多选择，他们选择了其他工作。花在休闲和娱乐上的时间比例随着时间的推移稳步增加。我认为随着技术的发展，这种情况将继续下去。基本需求将得到满足，人们可以自由地从事更多创造性工作，或者如果他们不想的话，不必那么辛苦地工作。但有些人会像我一样喜欢一直工作，你将能够比以往任何时候都完成更多的事情。这将是一种选择。

turns.en: Since you're a leader in innovation and technology, how do you know that your convictions are the best for everybody? How do you figure that out? It seems like such a challenge.
turns.zh: 既然你是创新和技术的领导者，你怎么知道你的信念对每个人都最好？你是怎么弄清楚的？这似乎是一个很大的挑战。

turns.en: At the end of the day, there are still a lot of options for people. Just because I build something doesn't mean people will use it. Some things work, some don't. Part of why the company has been successful is a slightly higher hit rate, but it's like baseball—most people don't get on base most of the time. More stuff doesn't work than does. If we do something that doesn't work, people generally won't use it, and the future doesn't go in that direction.
turns.zh: 归根结底，人们仍然有很多选择。我建造某样东西并不意味着人们会使用它。有些事情行得通，有些则不行。公司成功的一部分原因是成功率略高，但这就像棒球——大多数时候大多数人上不了垒。不成功的事情比成功的多。如果我们做了不成功的事情，人们通常不会使用它，未来也不会朝那个方向发展。

turns.en: So you're saying it's up to the user more.
turns.zh: 所以你是说这更取决于用户。

turns.en: One way to look at the world is that individual people are very powerful and have a lot of autonomy. There are narratives that try to diminish people's autonomy, but I've always believed that people are smarter than others think. People can make good decisions for their lives. When the media thinks something doesn't make sense, it's usually because they don't understand that person's life, not because people are stupid. If people say something that seems wrong, it's usually not misinformation—it's that you don't understand what's going on in their life. There's a certain paternalism in mainstream narratives, a know-it-allism. I think that's starting to change as cultural elites have a harder time predicting the world. The best predictor has always been: if you build something, do people actually think it's good? I believe people are very smart and understand their lives well. If you build something useful, they will use it. If not, they have other options. It's served me well to have faith in people. Whenever we adopt an attitude of 'we must know better than them,' that's when you lose. If you have that attitude long enough, you become a shitty company and become irrelevant. People are smarter than a lot of people think, and they ultimately drive the direction society goes in.
turns.zh: 看待世界的一种方式是，个体非常强大，拥有很大的自主权。有些叙事试图削弱人们的自主权，但我一直相信人们比其他人认为的更聪明。人们可以为自己的生活做出好的决定。当媒体认为某件事不合理时，通常是因为他们不了解那个人的生活，而不是因为人们愚蠢。如果人们说了看似错误的话，通常不是错误信息——而是你不了解他们生活中发生了什么。主流叙事中有一种家长式作风，一种自以为是的态度。我认为这种情况正在改变，因为文化精英们越来越难以预测世界。最好的预测指标一直是：如果你建造了某样东西，人们真的认为它好吗？我相信人们非常聪明，非常了解自己的生活。如果你建造了有用的东西，他们会使用它。如果没有用，他们有其他选择。相信人们一直对我很有帮助。每当我们采取‘我们一定比他们更懂’的态度时，那就是你失败的时候。如果你长期持有这种态度，你就会变成一家糟糕的公司，变得无关紧要。人们比很多人认为的更聪明，他们最终会推动社会发展的方向。

turns.en: People often say Elon Musk is socially awkward. I think we all are. Have you ever felt socially awkward over the years?
turns.zh: 人们常说埃隆·马斯克社交尴尬。我觉得我们都是。这些年来你感到过社交尴尬吗？

turns.en: No, I'm really smooth. Obviously yes. I'm the most awkward person. People have been calling me a robot online for 20 years. It does wonders for my confidence.
turns.zh: 不，我很圆滑。显然是的。我是最尴尬的人。人们在网上叫我机器人已经 20 年了。这对我的自信很有好处。

turns.en: Your confidence cannot be impaled. That's a sheer northstar inside you. You might have become bulletproof. You seem like a guy who watched a video on how to be a guy on YouTube. But we all go through awkwardness in different ways. Put you in certain environments, you're not at all. I haven't found those environments yet. But it's nice of you to be here today.
turns.zh: 你的自信不会被刺穿。那是你内心的一颗北极星。你可能已经变得刀枪不入了。你看起来像是一个在 YouTube 上看过如何做男人的视频的人。但我们都以不同的方式经历尴尬。把你放在某些环境中，你一点也不尴尬。我还没有找到那些环境。但今天你能来这里真是太好了。

transcript.sec: Social awkwardness and building social technology

turns.en: No, I think the podcasts are awesome because you get to explore something, right? But here's my question. Sorry, Mark, I don't mean to interrupt you, but I'm not going to get another chance to. So my question is, is it interesting that they have kind of people who would probably self-describe as socially awkward at times kind of creating technology that socially connects people? Do you ever find that kind of fascinating? Because I've always had a belief that sometimes socially awkward people are almost a mix between human and machine, like the future or something. Does that make any sense to you?
turns.zh: 不，我觉得播客很棒，因为你可以探索一些东西，对吧？但我的问题是——抱歉，马克，我不是故意打断你，但我没别的机会了。我的问题是，那些可能自认为有时社交尴尬的人，却在创造连接人们的社交技术，这难道不有趣吗？你有没有觉得这很迷人？因为我一直有种信念，有时社交尴尬的人几乎像是人类和机器的混合体，就像未来什么的。你觉得这有道理吗？

turns.en: Yeah, it's an interesting question. I think there are a bunch of factors here that you need to peel apart. Someone can be socially perceptive and understand what is going on in social dynamics and have a lot of empathy and care about other people while still being quite awkward in how they communicate. I don't think you can build a great... Yeah, I'm trying to build too broad of a bridge.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是个有趣的问题。我觉得这里有很多因素需要拆开来看。一个人可以有社交洞察力，理解社交动态中发生了什么，有很强的同理心并关心他人，同时在沟通方式上却相当笨拙。我不认为你能建起一座……嗯，我试图搭一座太宽的桥。

turns.en: No, I think it's a fair point. I mean, a lot of social media is people creating great content and communicating really well. And those are not my biggest strengths. I don't think I'm the best communicator by a long shot. I think I got to where I am because I understand what people like and I have the ability to build it, but I don't think my strength is communicating about why what I'm building is awesome. I generally like to let my work speak for itself. I try to explain how I'm thinking about it, but I don't think people primarily use our stuff because they saw me talk about it and thought, "Oh, this seems super exciting now." The ability to communicate in a way that is not awkward is a different skill than the ability to understand and have empathy for people and social interactions. There's an interesting thing where I actually think sometimes a lot of the people who can communicate in the smoothest way sometimes have a lot less empathy and understanding of social dynamics than the kind of nerdy guy who may not be able to express himself quite as well, but understands a little bit better what's going on. The world's complicated, there are multiple dimensions to all this stuff, and no one is good at all of them. So you just try to do the things you like, hone the things you're good at, and put it to service to do as good of work as you can.
turns.zh: 不，我觉得你说得有道理。我是说，很多社交媒体都是人们创造精彩内容并很好地沟通。而这些并不是我最大的强项。我远非最好的沟通者。我认为我能走到今天，是因为我理解人们喜欢什么，并且有能力把它建造出来，但我的强项不是沟通我建造的东西为什么很棒。我通常喜欢让作品自己说话。我试着解释我的思考方式，但我不认为人们主要因为看到我谈论它就觉得“哦，这看起来超级令人兴奋”才使用我们的产品。以不尴尬的方式沟通的能力，与理解和同情他人及社交互动的能力是不同的技能。有趣的是，我实际上认为，有时那些能以最流畅方式沟通的人，反而比那些不太能表达自己但更理解状况的书呆子型家伙，拥有更少的同理心和对社交动态的理解。世界很复杂，所有这些事情都有多个维度，没有人擅长所有方面。所以你只需做你喜欢的事，磨练你擅长的东西，并把它用于服务，尽你所能做好工作。

turns.en: Yeah. I think we're in this unique place where I believe it's one or two generations think that something isn't social and then the younger generation thinks that it is social. So I feel like in some senses we're at this crossroads of how we communicate as humans a little bit, and this advancement, and sometimes those steps are tricky to take. This is my last question because I know you have to go. I feel like Elon Musk wants to get on Mars and impregnate different planets or whatever he's doing. He's just blasting his seat out into the different rockets. He's like the Johnny Apple solar system. But God bless, 100%. I'm just joking. I think he probably would know that. I just think it's interesting you get what his ideas are. He wants to be on Mars, send rockets, make everything solar powered. You're such an innovator and a leader. What do you feel is your kind of goal, or where do you see us going? What is the thing where you see us at a point where you're really proud of and this is where you see us going? Because we're all on this bus together going somewhere, and we don't exactly know where we're going because it's the future. But you're kind of one of the guys driving the bus, or at least riding shotgun. So where are we going?
turns.zh: 是的。我认为我们处在一个独特的位置，我相信有一两代人认为某样东西不是社交性的，而年轻一代则认为它是社交性的。所以我觉得在某种意义上，我们正处在人类沟通方式的十字路口，这种进步有时很难迈出步伐。这是我最后一个问题，因为我知道你得走了。我觉得埃隆·马斯克想上火星，让其他星球受孕，或者他在做的任何事。他只是把自己的座位发射到不同的火箭里。他就像太阳系的约翰尼·苹果籽。但上帝保佑，100%。我只是开玩笑。我想他大概知道。我只是觉得有趣，你明白他的想法。他想上火星，发射火箭，让一切太阳能化。你是如此创新和领导力。你觉得你的目标是什么，或者你看到我们走向何方？你看到我们到达一个你真正自豪的点，并且你看到我们走向那里的是什么？因为我们都在这辆巴士上一起前往某个地方，我们并不确切知道要去哪里，因为那是未来。但你算是开车的人之一，至少坐在副驾驶。那么我们要去哪里？

turns.en: I think different people just care about different parts of the future. The space thing, I think it's cool. I'm glad that people are working on it. It's never really been my main thing. For me, it's all been about the intersection of how do you build technology that helps people connect with each other and understand the world better, and it's just taken different formats over time. You got started with writing text, then we got smartphones and cameras. We started taking a lot of photos and sharing that. Now most of what we do is video. The mobile networks are good enough that you can share great video. 10 years ago you try watching a video, it's buffering, terrible. But now it's good. People always want to both express their ideas and experience other people's ideas in whatever the richest format is that they can. If that was text, then that was the best they had. Photos, visual, great. A picture's worth a thousand words. Video better than photo for most things. But I don't think that's the end of the line. I think we're going to be here, whether it's 5 years or 10 years. The ability to fully capture moments, to really be able to experience them — that's the hologram thing we're talking about. That's going to be the next level of people being able to express ideas. Like the next blank canvas. So that's one thing. And then there's the whole AI thing which we haven't spent time on. That is really going to give people a lot of new tools to get smarter at everything they do. If you look at the world through this lens of how we express ourselves and how we take in an understanding of what's going on, AI is just going to be super powerful for both. You can already see some of the basic stuff with people creating images. It's unbelievable. It's crazy how fast it's happening.
turns.zh: 我认为不同的人只关心未来的不同部分。太空这件事，我觉得很酷。我很高兴有人在研究它。但这从来不是我的主要方向。对我来说，一切始终围绕着如何构建技术来帮助人们相互连接并更好地理解世界，而且它随着时间的推移采取了不同的形式。你从写文字开始，然后我们有了智能手机和摄像头。我们开始拍很多照片并分享。现在我们做的大部分是视频。移动网络足够好，你可以分享高质量视频。10 年前你试着看视频，它一直在缓冲，很糟糕。但现在好了。人们总是想以他们能获得的最丰富形式来表达自己的想法并体验他人的想法。如果是文字，那就是他们拥有的最好的。照片，视觉的，很棒。一图胜千言。视频在大多数事情上比照片更好。但我不认为这是终点。我认为我们会在那里，无论是 5 年还是 10 年。完全捕捉瞬间、真正体验它们的能力——这就是我们谈论的全息图。这将是人们表达想法的下一个层次。就像下一块空白画布。所以这是一件事。然后还有整个 AI 的事情，我们还没花时间聊。那真的会给人们很多新工具，让他们在做的每件事上都变得更聪明。如果你通过我们如何表达自己以及如何理解正在发生的事情这个视角来看世界，AI 对两者都将非常强大。你已经可以看到一些基本的东西，比如人们创建图像。这令人难以置信。它发生得如此之快，太疯狂了。

turns.en: That's what I'm amazed at is just how fast it's happening.
turns.zh: 这就是我惊讶的地方，就是它发生得如此之快。

transcript.secZh: 社交尴尬与构建社交科技

turns.en: But do you envision like a utopian idea? How do you see things? You're probably one of the smartest people to ever use thought. What is it like out there?
turns.zh: 但你是否设想了一个乌托邦式的愿景？你怎么看？你可能是最善于运用思想的人之一。外面是什么样子？

turns.en: I think we're going to get general intelligence. We'll have systems smarter than any individual. It's mostly going to be very empowering. There are already systems smarter than any one individual today, like a company with thousands of people working together. Instead of relatively few people harnessing that power, in the future almost everyone will have that. That means more ideas get tried out, leveling up creativity. It'll affect every field: science advances, productivity increases, and things get more fun and funny—memes get weirder and more specific. That's advancement too. It's like universal basic technology, the ability to express complex ideas in simple media. We'll get better at that, advancing our understanding of society. So I think we'll get superintelligence, continuing a hundred-year trend of more time for creative things and less drudgery. You'll have more powerful tools than ever imagined.
turns.zh: 我认为我们会获得通用智能。我们将拥有比任何个体都更聪明的系统。这主要会赋予人们更多力量。今天已经有比任何个人更聪明的系统，比如一个数千人协作的公司。未来，不是少数人利用这种力量，而是几乎每个人都能拥有。这意味着更多想法会被尝试，提升整体创造力。它会影响到每个领域：科学进步，生产力提高，事情变得更有趣、更好笑——梗变得更奇怪、更具体。那也是进步。这有点像通用基础技术，用简单媒介表达复杂想法的能力。我们会越来越擅长，推进我们对社会的理解。所以我认为我们会获得超级智能，延续人类一百多年来更多时间做创造性事情、更少苦差事的趋势。你将拥有比想象中更强大的工具。

turns.en: So it's not a conviction, but a space of choice?
turns.zh: 所以这不是一种信念，而是一个选择空间？

turns.en: What do you mean?
turns.zh: 什么意思？

turns.en: Like you're not convicted to a specific future; it's a space of choice. If you want to do these things, you can. There you are on the bus. That's a weird photo. I don't think that's good. Horrible AI. I think that was OpenAI. Sorry about that.
turns.zh: 就像你并不执着于某个特定的未来；它是一个选择空间。如果你想做这些事，你就可以做。你在公交车上那张照片有点怪。我觉得那不好。可怕的 AI。我觉得那是 OpenAI 的。抱歉。

turns.en: I don't like that at all.
turns.zh: 我一点也不喜欢那个。

turns.en: Do you think you'll live forever, Mark? Is that a thought coming to your head?
turns.zh: 你觉得你会永生吗，马克？你有这个想法吗？

turns.en: I don't know. Live forever. At some point we will. Let me come at it this way. Outside of Meta, the philanthropy I do with Priscilla through the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative is focused on curing diseases. We're not focusing on any single disease, but on basic technology at the intersection of AI and biology to accelerate science. We originally thought a hundred years was the timeframe to cure, prevent, and deal with all diseases. I think there's a chance that happens sooner because of AI. I'm more optimistic now. Whether that means living forever or just healthier lives for the period we're supposed to live, I don't think we understand that yet. But curing diseases is not just about living forever; it's about having better lives while you're alive—not feeling terrible from sickness or injury.
turns.zh: 我不知道。永生。总有一天会的。我换个方式说吧。在 Meta 之外，我和普莉希拉通过 Chan Zuckerberg Initiative 做的慈善主要关注治愈疾病。我们不是专注于某一种疾病，而是关注 AI 和生物学交叉的基础技术，以加速科学进步。我们最初认为一百年是一个时间框架来治愈、预防和处理所有疾病。我认为由于 AI，这有可能更快发生。我现在更乐观了。这是否意味着永生，或者只是在我们应该活着的时期过更健康的生活，我认为我们还不了解。但治愈疾病不仅仅关乎永生；它关乎在你活着的时候拥有更好的生活——不因疾病或受伤而感觉糟糕。

turns.en: Do you think it's possible we could figure out curing all diseases to keep life going?
turns.zh: 你认为我们有可能找到治愈所有疾病的方法来延续生命吗？

turns.en: I think it's possible. Honestly, it's not an area I've studied that much. We don't know what's going on. Let's check back in 10 years. As AI makes more progress, we'll get a sense of the trajectory. I think it's going to unlock a lot of creativity, productivity, and fun. The technology industry misses fun a lot. People are focused on making a better word processor or processing information better. But a lot of what people care about is being entertained, having fun, showing funny things to friends, and hanging out. AI is going to make all that great. That's a big focus for us at Meta AI—we call it personal AI.
turns.zh: 我认为有可能。老实说，这不是我深入研究过的领域。我们不知道会发生什么。十年后再看吧。随着 AI 取得更多进展，我们会看到轨迹。我认为它会释放大量创造力、生产力和乐趣。科技行业常常忽略了乐趣。人们专注于制作更好的文字处理器或更高效地处理信息。但人们真正关心的是娱乐、乐趣、给朋友看有趣的东西、一起玩。AI 会让这一切变得很棒。这是我们 Meta AI 的重点——我们称之为个人 AI。

transcript.sec: Building a fun and personal AI

turns.en: I mean, our goal on this is not just to build something that's super smart. We're trying to solve full general intelligence and superintelligence, but to build a product people want to use, you need something fun. That means not just typing to it, but having a conversation. It's not only about answering hard questions; it should get to know you, what you think is funny, what you ate, your hobbies, so it can relate to you.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我们的目标不只是构建一个超级智能的东西。我们正在尝试解决完整的通用智能和超级智能，但要构建一个人们愿意使用的产品，你需要一些有趣的东西。这意味着不只是打字，而是进行对话。它不仅仅要能回答难题，还应该了解你、你觉得什么好笑、你吃了什么、你的爱好，这样它才能与你产生共鸣。

turns.en: I think people don't know that AI can do that. We're missing education on what AI is and how to use it. I noticed even in my own life, people aren't understanding what's going on. So I don't know how we get people educated quickly. Whoever can serve people the best way to educate themselves will be able to best coagulate people to their AI model. A lot of people say, 'We have the best AI,' but most people are like, 'What the hell are we doing?'
turns.zh: 我认为人们不知道 AI 能做到这一点。我们缺少关于 AI 是什么以及如何使用它的教育。我注意到即使在我自己的生活中，人们也不理解正在发生的事情。所以我不知道如何让人们快速接受教育。谁能最好地帮助人们自我教育，谁就能最好地把人们凝聚到他们的 AI 模型上。很多人说“我们有最好的 AI”，但大多数人都在想“我们到底在干什么？”

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So, anyway, sorry. I think I kept you longer than we were supposed to. We got on a lot of technological stuff. Sometimes we don't know what's happening and we want to talk to somebody like you. It's important.
turns.zh: 所以，总之，抱歉。我想我让你待得比预定时间长了。我们聊了很多技术方面的东西。有时候我们不知道发生了什么，我们想和你这样的人聊聊。这很重要。

turns.en: No, it's good. This is a fun conversation.
turns.zh: 不，没关系。这是一次有趣的对话。

turns.en: Yeah, man. I enjoyed it, too. Thanks for sharing just some of what your life is like with us. I hope just keep make sure we stay alive or whatever if we're supposed to and just keep working on it. Okay, that's all we can ask for now, sir. Awesome. Mark, thanks. Appreciate it, brother.
turns.zh: 是的，兄弟。我也很享受。谢谢你跟我们分享你生活的一部分。我希望我们继续确保我们活着，或者不管怎样，继续努力。好了，这就是我们现在能要求的全部了，先生。太棒了。马克，谢谢。感激不尽，兄弟。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Now I'm just floating on the breeze and I feel I'm falling like these leaves. I must be corner stone. Oh, but when I reach that ground, I'll share this peace of mind I found. I can feel it in my bones. Also want to say a thank you to the people at Grace Dental. That's Grace Dental in Palo Alto, California. They glued my tooth was broken and they glued it back together before the interview today. So, I'm thankful that they helped and I'm grateful that I got to meet them.
turns.zh: 现在我随风飘荡，感觉自己像这些落叶一样飘落。我一定是基石。哦，但当我到达地面时，我会分享我找到的这份内心的平静。我能从骨子里感觉到。还想感谢 Grace Dental 的人们。那是加州帕洛阿尔托的 Grace Dental。我的牙齿断了，他们在今天的采访前帮我粘好了。所以，我很感激他们的帮助，也很高兴能见到他们。
